quick_facts.label: Credential
quick_facts.value: MBA (Master of Business Administration) is a specified master’s-degree title in India.

quick_facts.label: Typical format
quick_facts.value: The canonical full-time MBA is commonly a two-year postgraduate programme; for example, IIM Bangalore’s PGP leads to an MBA degree and is a two-year, full-time residential programme.

quick_facts.label: Entry qualification
quick_facts.value: Requirements are institution-specific, but a bachelor’s degree is the standard entry base. For its 2026–28 MBA, IIM Bangalore requires a bachelor’s degree in any discipline with at least 50% marks/equivalent CGPA (45% for SC, ST and PwD applicants).

quick_facts.label: Admissions
quick_facts.value: Admission-test routes vary by institution: IIM Ahmedabad uses CAT for domestic applicants and GMAT for overseas Indian applicants/foreign nationals; XLRI’s management admission route uses XAT for its PGDM programmes.

quick_facts.label: Fees are highly variable
quick_facts.value: As dated institutional context, IIM Ahmedabad listed a programme fee of INR 27,50,000 for its 2025–27 PGP batch, while stating that fees for future batches would be announced later; this is not a nationwide MBA fee benchmark.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: An MBA is a postgraduate route open to graduates from varied academic backgrounds, rather than only commerce or business graduates. IIM Bangalore’s 2026–28 MBA admission criteria illustrate the common pattern: a bachelor’s degree in any discipline, at least 50% marks or equivalent CGPA, with a 45% threshold for SC, ST and PwD applicants, plus a valid CAT score. Each institution sets its own exact minimum marks, category rules, accepted qualifications and work-experience expectations.

The broader UGC master’s-degree framework historically requires completion of an undergraduate degree for admission to a master’s programme. Applicants should therefore check the current prospectus of every target institution instead of treating one school’s percentage threshold as universal.
bullets: A prior degree may be from arts, commerce, science, engineering, medicine, design, law or another recognized discipline, subject to the admitting institution’s rules.
Final-year applicants may be permitted by particular schools, but completion-document and minimum-mark conditions are institution-specific.
Work experience is not universally required for the standard two-year MBA; it is often more important or mandatory for executive/one-year MBA formats.

sections.heading: Duration and study mode
paragraphs: For the canonical Indian MBA node, the usual format is a two-year postgraduate management degree. IIM Bangalore describes its PGP MBA as two-year, full-time and residential; IIM Ahmedabad likewise describes its flagship MBA/PGP as a two-year programme.

This should not be confused with every programme carrying the MBA name. IIM Ahmedabad, for example, separately offers a one-year MBA-PGPX for experienced professionals and a two-year blended postgraduate management degree. Prospective students should compare the award, mode, work-experience requirement, placement access and total fee rather than assuming that all MBAs are interchangeable.
bullets: Typical canonical route: two academic years after a bachelor’s degree.
Common structure: classroom terms in year one, a summer internship between years, and electives/specialisation choices in year two.
Variants may be one-year, executive, online or blended and can have materially different admissions criteria.

sections.heading: Curriculum and learning experience
paragraphs: MBA curricula normally establish a common management foundation before allowing greater choice. IIM Ahmedabad’s MBA curriculum, for example, makes the first year compulsory across accounting and finance, behavioural science, communication, economics, information technology, marketing, personnel management and industrial relations, operations management, law, strategy and quantitative methods. It requires an eight-to-ten-week organisational internship after the first year, while the second year enables study in areas of special interest alongside compulsory context courses.

The exact mix of cases, lectures, projects, live industry work, exchange opportunities, specialisations and assessment differs by school. At IIM Ahmedabad, the programme describes a first-year core and second-year electives; it reports approximately 173 elective courses as an example of how broad second-year choice can be at a large business school.
bullets: Core managerial domains commonly include finance/accounting, marketing, operations, economics, strategy, organisational behaviour, communication, law and quantitative analysis.
A summer internship commonly links the first and second years, but duration and assessment rules vary.
Second-year electives can support general management or functional depth such as finance, marketing, operations, strategy, analytics or entrepreneurship.

sections.heading: Admission routes and selection
paragraphs: There is no single all-India MBA admission process. At IIM Ahmedabad, domestic applicants are assessed through CAT followed by an analytical writing test and personal interview for shortlisted candidates; selection also considers academic record and other achievements. IIM Bangalore’s 2026–28 MBA process likewise requires a valid CAT score in addition to its academic eligibility criteria.

Other established routes exist. XLRI uses XAT for admission to its management PGDM programmes. Some university postgraduate admission systems use CUET-PG: for the University of Delhi’s 2026–27 two-year postgraduate admissions, allocation is through its CSAS(PG) process using CUET-PG 2026 scores and the programme-specific eligibility rules. GMAT is also used by some Indian MBA routes, including IIM Ahmedabad’s overseas Indian/foreign-national admission category. Test acceptance, score validity, cut-offs, interview stages, reservation rules and deadlines must be verified in the relevant year’s official admission notice.
bullets: CAT: central route for many IIM two-year MBA/PGP programmes, but each IIM applies its own shortlist and final-selection policy.
XAT: key route for XLRI’s management PGDM programmes and accepted by some other management schools.
GMAT: commonly relevant for international, overseas Indian, executive or school-specific routes; acceptance is institution-dependent.
CUET-PG: used by some university postgraduate systems; it is not a universal MBA entrance test.

sections.heading: Costs and financial planning
paragraphs: MBA costs vary sharply by institution, city, programme format and whether accommodation and course materials are bundled. As a dated example rather than a market-wide estimate, IIM Ahmedabad listed INR 27,50,000 for its 2025–27 PGP batch, including tuition of INR 20,10,000; it explicitly excluded several personal expenses and stated that fees for future batches would be announced later.

Applicants should budget beyond headline tuition: application and test fees, deposits, hostel/board where not bundled, laptop and living costs, travel, insurance and the opportunity cost of leaving employment. Check the school’s current fee schedule, payment calendar, refund rules and scholarship/loan information before accepting an offer.
bullets: Use only the current batch’s official fee page or admission offer for decision-making.
Compare what is included: tuition, academic materials, board/hostel, international module, placement services and refundable deposits may be treated differently.
Scholarships and financial assistance can be school-specific; IIM Ahmedabad publishes financial-assistance information alongside its MBA admissions material.

sections.heading: What an MBA can lead to
paragraphs: An MBA can support management and business career transitions, but it is not a guaranteed job outcome. As dated placement context, IIM Bangalore reported that in its 2026 final and lateral placement cycle, management consulting accounted for 45% of offers, technology/product management 15%, and investment banking 11%. This is evidence from one highly selective institution and one placement cycle—not a salary or placement forecast for all MBA programmes or students.

Common post-MBA directions therefore include management consulting, investment banking and other finance roles, product management, general-management/management-trainee roles, marketing, operations, entrepreneurship and further study. Candidates targeting investment work may also consider the CFA Program: CFA Institute states that the credential involves three exam levels, with bachelor’s degree/work-experience entry routes and additional work-experience requirements for the charter. Management research is another route; IIM Bangalore maintains a doctoral programme with its own admissions process and requirements.
bullets: Employment: role availability depends on prior experience, school, internship performance, market conditions and employer selection—not only the MBA title.
Finance specialisation: the CFA Program can complement, rather than replace, an MBA for candidates pursuing investment-oriented work.
Research: doctoral/FPM/PhD routes have separate test, academic-record, interview and research-fit requirements.
Entrepreneurship: an MBA may build management and network capabilities, but starting a venture does not require the degree.
